text: type of ad you come across when you just got your meta ad account banned😂

Saw this and the angle alone is worth saving.  

"Did you know low estrogen can cause micro-tears in your rotator cuff? There is a high concentration of estrogen receptors in the rotator cuff tendons which are responsible for maintaining tendon strength and integrity."  

The hook is a mechanism most readers have never heard. shoulder pain is usually framed as aging or overuse, and the buyer has already been told that by a doctor. introducing estrogen as the missing variable creates a wait-what moment that almost guarantees the next scroll is downward.  

Then the symptom list reads like a diagnostic checklist. outer arm pain, overhead movement pain, clicking, range of motion loss. the buyer who has at least two of those is now mentally raising their hand. you cannot scroll away once you've identified with three of five symptoms in a row.  

The 48% stat anchors the claim in research-flavored language. one number is enough. the moment you stack three or four, the ad starts sounding like an academic paper and the conversion drops.  

The disqualification block is the real conversion engine. it names painkillers, massages, chiropractors, glucosamine, collagen, and explains why each one fails. that is every previous attempt the buyer has made, listed back to them with a single reason they didn't work. by the time provitalize gets introduced, the buyer has already accepted the diagnosis.  

The no-HRT line is the relief valve. it preempts the biggest objection in the menopause supplement space.  been running 24 days.

The ad isn't selling a fungus solution, it's selling access to a secret. Big difference

This brand is running hinglish copy and it's the right call for the audience.  

"Aaj ke oily food, stress & irregular lifestyle ki wajah se acidity, gas, bloating aur constipation common ho chuka hai."  

Mixing hindi and english in the same sentence is how the target buyer actually talks, and ads that match the inner voice of the reader always outperform translated copy. the writer here understood that an indian middle-class buyer scrolling reels at night is not reading polished english, they're reading the way they think.  

The problem stack at the top is broad on purpose. acidity, gas, bloating, constipation. four conditions covered in one sentence widens the audience without diluting the offer, because the underlying product is a digestion juice that targets all four.  

The ayurvedic ingredient list does the credibility work. amla, harad, baheda, sonth, pipali. these are heritage words that a north-indian buyer recognizes from childhood. you cannot fake that vocabulary. it signals that the brand is rooted in tradition, not imported from a us supplement template.  

The second-half pivot to lightness and energy is the aspirational close. relief is the floor, but feeling energetic through the day is the ceiling the buyer is actually after. naming both gives the ad a wider emotional reach than a pure relief pitch.  

Format choice with the green leaf, the cart emoji, the structured checkmarks reads like a vernacular feed-native ad, not a brand template.

The best health ads don't educate first, they make the right person feel seen
bro's meta ads knowledge after reading this:

Scrolling and this one stopped me with the testimonial choice.  

"60% OFF Sale Ends Today! Relieved my carpal tunnel within 2 weeks. 15 mins 3 times daily. Also helps with arthritis. Jim F."  

The structure is doing three jobs at once. urgency on top, social proof in the middle, guarantee at the bottom. that's a complete buying frame in twenty words, no fluff, no setup.  

What makes the testimonial work is the specificity. two weeks. fifteen minutes. three times daily. real numbers from a real-sounding name. fake testimonials almost always avoid concrete protocols because they don't know what the product actually requires. jim's testimonial reads like he actually used it.  

The also-helps-with-arthritis tag at the end of the quote is sneaky-good. it expands the addressable audience without making the ad about arthritis. anyone scrolling who has hand pain that isn't carpal tunnel now has a reason to keep reading.  

The 90-day guarantee at the bottom is the safety net that lets the buyer click on a name they've never heard of before. free shipping plus refund flips the risk back onto the brand, which is the only way unknown ecom brands move impulse purchases in a pain category.  

Sale ends today is the oldest trick in the book and it still works because the buyer knows they'll forget by tomorrow.

Found this local hospital ad and it's a useful study in trust-stacking for healthcare.  

"15+ Yrs experienced surgeons. 60000+ Happy Patients. Lower complications & higher success rates. All Insurance Accepted. 0% Interest EMI. End to end patient care. Free pick & drop services."  

Every line is a single objection neutralized. experience covers competence doubt. 60,000 patients cover volume doubt. insurance and emi cover cost doubt. pickup covers logistics doubt. the ad reads like a checklist of every reason someone would not call, with a counter for each one.  

The phone number sitting in the hook is the actual conversion driver. healthcare ads that try to push a form-fill underperform brands that just put a number at the top. the older buyer dialing from a parked car is the actual audience, not someone filling out a multi-step quiz.  

Minimally invasive in the headline is the product positioning. kidney stones is the search term. bangalore is the geo. all three keywords land in the first sentence, which is how this thing keeps reaching the right intent.  

The green checkmarks do scanning work. on a phone feed, that visual pattern makes the bullets feel like guarantees instead of features. small format choice, real lift on read-through.  

Healthcare creatives rarely need to be clever. they need to remove friction and signal volume. this one does both without wasting a word.  

Been running 32 days.

This hit different because it opens at the surgery line and works backwards.  

"I genuinely thought I was heading toward surgery. The pain on the outside of my hip got so bad I couldn't sleep on that side anymore. I had to put a pillow between my knees just to get through the night."  

Stakes first. surgery is the worst-case outcome the reader is privately fearing and naming it in the first sentence creates immediate identification. the pillow between the knees detail does what generic pain copy can never do. it's a specific behavior every menopausal woman with hip pain has tried.  

Then the unmasking. gluteal tendinopathy gets named. estrogen support for tendons gets explained. an OB-GYN delivers the line that previous doctors missed. that handoff from suffering to diagnosis is the spine of the entire ad.  the failed-attempts list is the proof. turmeric, collagen, painkillers, physio. she rules out every cheaper option before introducing the product, which preempts the buyer's i already tried that objection.  

The product mention comes late and sounds like a tip from a friend, not a pitch. moringa phytoestrogens with curcumin. specific ingredient pairing, named brand, no superlatives. that restraint is what makes it read like a story instead of a sales letter.  

The last line about not imagining it is doing validation work that converts. women in this market have been gaslit by their own doctors for years.  been running 15 days.

they win because the viewer understands the problem before they read the label

this concept works because it doesn’t lead with “daily support”

not capsules
not ingredients
not vague wellness copy
not a pretty product shot

it leads with the inside visual

today creates fear
